hero.title: Documentation
hero.subtitle: Everything you need to build reactive web apps with No.JS

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.text: No.JS is an HTML-first reactive framework. Build dynamic, data-driven web applications using nothing but HTML attributes — no build step, no virtual DOM, no JSX.
introduction.callout: Zero dependencies · Works in all modern browsers · No build step required

installation.title: Installation
installation.selfHostedText: Download dist/iife/no.js and include it with a <script> tag. It's a single file.

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.text: Create an index.html file: include the script, add some attributes, and you're done. No app.mount(), no createApp(), no NgModule. It just works.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.text: On DOMContentLoaded, No.JS walks the DOM looking for elements with known attributes. Each attribute maps to a directive that is executed by priority.
howItWorks.card1Desc: Walks the DOM looking for elements with known attributes.
howItWorks.card2Desc: Each attribute maps to a directive executed by priority (data fetching first, then conditionals, then rendering).
howItWorks.card3Desc: All data lives in reactive contexts (Proxy-backed). When data changes, every bound element updates automatically.
howItWorks.card4Desc: Contexts inherit from parent elements, like lexical scoping. A bind inside an each loop can access both the loop item and ancestor data.

coreConcepts.title: Core Concepts
coreConcepts.reactiveContextText: Every element can have a context — a reactive data object. Contexts are created by state, get, store, etc. Child elements inherit their parent's context automatically.
coreConcepts.tableRow1b: Fetch data, error boundaries, i18n namespace
coreConcepts.tableRow15: Drag and drop setup (Now in NoJS Elements)
coreConcepts.expressionText: Most directive values accept JavaScript expressions evaluated against the current context:

data.title: Data
data.base: Set API base URL for descendants
data.as: Name for fetched data in context
data.into: Write response to a named global store
data.success: Template ID to show on success
data.skeleton: Show/hide a placeholder element during loading
data.sseInsert: How messages update the variable (replace, append, prepend)
data.sseLimit: Maximum array length in append/prepend mode

state.title: State
state.persist: Attribute of state directive — persists state to storage
state.persistSchema: Validate restored keys against initial state

loops.title: Loops
loops.from: Source array (DEPRECATED — use "item in array" syntax instead)
loops.sort: Sort property (name only, not prefixed with item variable)
loops.else: Template rendered when the array is empty or null/undefined

events.title: Events
events.self: Only fires if event target is the element itself

forms.title: Forms
forms.validate: Enable form/field validation (Now in NoJS Elements)

routing.title: Routing
routing.routeActiveExact: Exact match active class for route links
routing.lazyPriority: Load remote template before all others (Phase 0)
routing.lazyOnDemand: Fetch route template only when first visited (route templates only)
routing.routeMatched: true if an explicit route matched, false for wildcard/fallback
routing.i18nNs: Auto-derive i18n namespace from route filename
routing.transitionVT: View Transition API preset on route-view (slide, fade, scale, none)

animation.title: Animation
animation.transitionVT: View Transition API preset on route-view (slide, fade, scale, none)

dnd.title: Drag and Drop (Now in NoJS Elements)
dnd.dragGroup: Scope drag to a named group
dnd.dropSettleClass: Custom CSS class for settle animation
dnd.dropEmptyClass: Custom CSS class for empty state on drag-list
dnd.dragList: Sortable list bound to state array
dnd.dragListCopy: Copy instead of move on transfer
dnd.dragListRemove: Remove items from source on transfer

misc.title: Misc
misc.loading: Placeholder shown while remote template loads; removed on arrival
misc.include: Synchronously clone an inline template into the current position

state.title: state — Local State
state.text: Creates a reactive context scoped to the element and its children.

store.title: store — Global Store
store.text: A global reactive store accessible from anywhere. Ideal for auth state, theme, shared data.

configStores.title: Pre-initializing Stores via config()
configStores.text: You can pre-create stores with initial data inside NoJS.config(). Each key in the stores object becomes a named global store, accessible immediately via $store.name.
configStores.callout: If a store already exists (e.g. created via a store attribute), config() will not overwrite it — the existing store is preserved.

into.title: into — Write Fetch Results to a Store
into.text: The into attribute on any HTTP directive writes the response directly into a named global store.
into.callout: The store doesn't need to be pre-defined — into will create it if it doesn't exist.

computed.title: computed — Derived State
computed.text: Values that are automatically recalculated when dependencies change.

watch.title: watch — Side Effects
watch.text: Execute an action whenever a value changes.

persistence.title: State Persistence
persistence.text: Persist state across page reloads using localStorage or sessionStorage.

persistFields.title: persist-fields — Selective Persistence
persistFields.text: Use persist-fields to control exactly which fields are saved and restored. Fields not listed are never written to storage, which is useful for keeping sensitive values (tokens, passwords) out of localStorage/sessionStorage.
persistFields.text2: persist-fields accepts a comma-separated list of field names. Whitespace around each name is trimmed, so "theme, sidebar" and "theme,sidebar" are equivalent.
persistFields.callout: Omit persist-fields to persist all fields. When present, only the listed fields are saved — all other fields use their initial values on restore.

persistSchema.title: persist-schema — Schema Validation
persistSchema.text: When restoring persisted state, persist-schema validates that restored keys match the initial state shape. Keys not present in the initial state are discarded. This prevents stale or corrupted data from breaking your app after schema changes.

scoping.title: Context Scoping & Shadowing
scoping.text: Contexts inherit from parent elements like lexical scoping. A child state can shadow a parent property — the child sees its own value, while siblings see the parent's.
scoping.callout: Shadowing is intentional and follows JavaScript's scoping rules. If you need shared state across siblings, use a store instead.

notify.title: NoJS.notify() — Flush Store Updates
notify.text: When external JavaScript mutates a store via NoJS.store, call NoJS.notify() to flush all pending DOM updates.
notify.callout: Only needed when mutating NoJS.store from plain JavaScript — outside of HTML expressions. Store mutations inside on:click or bind expressions are handled automatically.

bind.title: bind — Text Content
bind.text: Replaces the element's textContent with the evaluated expression.
bind.filtersNote: Expressions inside bind support pipe filters to transform values for display:

bindHtml.title: bind-html — Inner HTML
bindHtml.text: Renders evaluated expression as HTML. Sanitized by default.
bindHtml.callout: ⚠️ Uses built-in DOMParser-based structural sanitization: strips <script> tags, blocks on* event handlers, and removes javascript: URIs.
bindHtml.sanitizerTip: Tip: You can provide a custom sanitizer via NoJS.config({ sanitizeHtml: fn }). See Configuration → Security for details.

model.title: model — Two-Way Binding
model.text: For form inputs, model creates automatic two-way data binding.

radioSelect.title: Radio Buttons & Multi-Select
radioSelect.text: model works with all input types. For radio buttons, bind the same model name to a group — the value updates to the selected radio's value.
radioSelect.multiSelectWarning: ⚠️ Multi-select model binding is not yet implemented. The current model directive uses el.value for <select> elements and does not read selectedOptions or handle the multiple attribute. An array-backed model will write back a single string value, not an array of selected values. This feature is planned for a future release. The example below is shown for reference only.

commonMistakes.title: Common Mistakes
commonMistakes.text: Avoid these common data binding pitfalls:
commonMistakes.mistake1: Using bind-html without understanding sanitization — all content is sanitized by default, but be aware of what gets stripped.
commonMistakes.mistake2: Forgetting that bind replaces textContent — any child elements inside a bind target are removed.
commonMistakes.mistake3: Using model on non-form elements — model only works on input, textarea, and select elements.

handlers.title: on:* — Event Handlers
handlers.text: Bind any DOM event directly in HTML. Access state and context variables directly in the handler expression.

modifiers.title: Event Modifiers
modifiers.text: Modifiers let you control event behavior directly in the attribute:
modifiers.once: Handler fires only once, then is automatically removed
modifiers.self: Only fires if event.target is the element itself
modifiers.debounce: Debounce the handler execution (append delay in ms, e.g. .debounce.300)
modifiers.throttle: Throttle the handler execution (append interval in ms, e.g. .throttle.200)

eventAndEl.title: $event & $el
eventAndEl.text: $event is the native DOM event. $el refers to the current element.

keyModifiers.title: Key Modifiers
keyModifiers.text: Use key modifiers on keyboard events to filter by specific keys. Combine with Ctrl, Alt, Shift, or Meta for shortcuts.
keyModifiers.meta: Meta (Cmd on Mac, Win on Windows)

ifThenElse.title: if / then / else
ifThenElse.text: Conditionally render elements or templates based on expressions.

showHide.title: show / hide
showHide.text: Toggles display: none without adding/removing DOM elements. Better for frequently toggled elements.

switchCase.title: switch / case
switchCase.text: Render one of many templates based on a value.

elseOnLoops.title: else on Loop Elements
elseOnLoops.text: Loop directives (foreach, each, for) support else="templateId" to reference a <template> for empty-state fallback. The template renders when the list is empty ([]) or null/undefined/non-array — e.g. API state before the first fetch.
elseOnLoops.callout: The sibling else pattern for loops was removed in v1.15. Use else="templateId" on the loop element itself. See the Loops page for details.

foreach.title: foreach — Iterate Over Arrays
foreach.text: The primary iteration directive. Place foreach="item in array" on the repeating element itself (self-repeating pattern). The element and its children are cloned for each item. each and for are aliases with identical behavior.
foreach.from: Source array (DEPRECATED — use item in array syntax)
foreach.key: Unique key expression for element identification and tracking
foreach.sort: Property path to sort by (prefix - for descending)
foreach.limit: Maximum number of items to render
foreach.template: External template ID (optional — children become template if omitted)
foreach.elseTpl: Template ID rendered when the array is empty or null/undefined (e.g. else="noItemsTpl")

fullExample.title: Full Example with All Attributes
fullExample.text: A complete example showing foreach with filtering, sorting, pagination, and an else template for the empty-state fallback.

aliases.title: Aliases: each and for
aliases.text: each and for are aliases of foreach — they share the same handler and support all the same attributes.

inline.title: Self-Repeating Pattern
inline.text: Place the loop directive on the repeating element itself. The element is cloned for each item in the array. Use else="templateId" to reference a template for the empty state.

deprecated.title: Deprecated: from Attribute
deprecated.text: The from attribute still works but is deprecated. Use the "item in array" syntax instead. Using from will emit a console warning.

nested.title: Nested Loops
nested.text: Child loops (foreach, each, or for) can access parent scope variables.

reactivity.title: Reactivity
reactivity.text: Loop directives are fully reactive. When the source array changes (items added, removed, or reordered), the DOM updates automatically. Use the key attribute for efficient diffing — without it, the entire list re-renders on every change.

objectIteration.title: Object Iteration
objectIteration.text: To iterate over an object's entries, use the keys or values filter to convert it to an array first.
objectIteration.callout: Direct object iteration is not supported — always convert to an array with keys, values, or Object.entries() in a computed expression.

elseTpl.title: Else with Template Reference
elseTpl.text: Place else="templateId" directly on the loop element. When the list is empty ([]) or null/undefined, the referenced template content replaces the loop output.
elseTpl.callout: The else attribute on the loop element activates for empty arrays ([]) and also for null, undefined, or non-array sources — e.g. API state before the first fetch resolves.
elseTpl.breaking: Breaking change (v1.15): the sibling else pattern for loops has been removed. Use else="templateId" on the loop element instead.

keyReconciliation.title: Key-Based Reconciliation
keyReconciliation.text: By default, when the source array changes, the loop directive performs a full rebuild — all child nodes are disposed and recreated from scratch. When you supply a key attribute, the directive switches to key-based reconciliation: existing DOM nodes for items whose key is still in the list are reused and their context is updated in place. Only items that genuinely appeared or disappeared trigger DOM mutations.
keyReconciliation.callout: The key value must be unique and stable across renders — typically a database ID or UUID. Using a non-unique key (e.g. $index) defeats reconciliation since items will always appear to match.
keyReconciliation.largeLists: Large lists (50+ items) with frequent updates
keyReconciliation.mutatingArrays: push / splice / sort on reactive arrays
keyReconciliation.useKeyPreserve: Use key to preserve focus, scroll, and input state
keyReconciliation.embeddedInputs: Items with embedded inputs, video, canvas
keyReconciliation.useKeyReset: Use key — full rebuild resets state
keyReconciliation.positionalText: After a reorder (e.g. sort), the reconciler calls $notify() on the context of each retained clone. This propagates the updated $index, $first, $last, $even, $odd, and $count values to all child watchers, including nested bindings and class expressions that depend on position.

externalTemplate.title: External Template
externalTemplate.text: Use the template attribute to reference a <template> element by ID. The loop element is still the repeating unit — clones of the referenced template are inserted as siblings.

animationAttrs.title: Loop Animation Attributes
animationAttrs.text: All loop directives (foreach, each, for) support enter/leave animations and stagger via these attributes:
animationAttrs.enter: CSS class added to each new item on insert
animationAttrs.leave: CSS class added to items before removal
animationAttrs.stagger: Delay (ms) between each item's enter animation
animationAttrs.duration: Max duration (ms) before leave animation is force-completed

basic.title: Basic Template
basic.text: Templates are reusable HTML fragments that are never rendered directly. They are cloned when referenced by directives like then, else, template, loading, error, etc.

var.title: Template Variables (var)
var.text: Templates can declare which variable they expect from the calling context.

slots.title: Template Slots
slots.text: Allow templates to accept projected content.

remote.title: Remote Templates (src)
remote.text: Load templates from external HTML files.

recursive.subtitle: Recursive Loading
recursive.text: Remote templates are loaded recursively — if a remote template itself contains <template src="..."> elements, those are automatically resolved too:

remoteRoutes.subtitle: Remote Templates in Routes
remoteRoutes.text: Remote templates inside route content are also automatically resolved before the route renders. See Routing for details.

lazy.title: Lazy Loading (lazy)
lazy.text: Control when remote templates are fetched using the lazy attribute on <template src="..."> elements. NoJS loads templates in phases to optimise time-to-first-render.
lazy.absentDesc: Default auto-prioritisation: content-include templates and the current route template load before first render; other route templates are preloaded in the background after first render.
lazy.priorityDesc: Force load before everything else — even before regular content includes. Useful for critical shared layout templates.
lazy.ondemandDesc: Only valid on route templates. Never preloaded — fetched lazily the first time the user navigates to that route. Ideal for heavy or rarely-visited pages.

phases.subtitle: Loading Phases
phases.text: Templates are resolved in four ordered phases: Phase 0 fetches lazy="priority" templates first; Phase 1 fetches all other non-route templates plus the active route template (blocking before first render); Phase 2 preloads remaining route templates in the background after first render; and on-demand fetches lazy="ondemand" route templates only when the user first navigates to them.

loading.title: Loading Placeholder (loading)
loading.text1: Show a placeholder template while a remote template is being fetched. The placeholder is inserted synchronously — before any network request — and removed automatically once the real content arrives. Works for both static content-includes and nested templates inside route pages.
loading.text2: Both plain IDs and #id syntax are accepted. The placeholder template is cloned each time, so it can be reused across multiple remote templates:

include.title: Inline Template Include (include)
include.text1: Clone an inline template into the current position synchronously, before any fetches. Useful for injecting reusable markup (e.g. icon sets, common fragments) without making a network request.
include.text2: include and loading serve different purposes: include clones inline content permanently; loading inserts a temporary placeholder that disappears once a remote template finishes loading.

baseUrl.title: Base URL
baseUrl.text1: Set once on any ancestor element. All descendant get, post, etc. resolve relative URLs against it.

get.title: get — Fetch and Render Data
get.as: Name to assign the response in the context. Default: "data"
get.loading: Template ID to show while loading (e.g. "#skeleton")
get.error: Template ID to show on fetch error
get.empty: Template ID to show when response is empty array/null
get.cached: Cache responses. cached = memory, cached="local" = localStorage, cached="session" = sessionStorage
get.into: Write response to a named global store
get.debounce: Debounce in ms (useful with reactive URLs)
get.params: Expression that resolves to query params object

reactiveUrls.title: Reactive URLs
reactiveUrls.text: URLs that reference state variables re-fetch automatically when those values change.

mutations.title: post, put, patch, delete — Mutating Requests
mutations.text: Used on forms or triggered via call.

mutationAttrs.title: Mutation Attributes
mutationAttrs.body: Request body (JSON string with interpolation). For forms, auto-serializes fields
mutationAttrs.success: Template ID to render on success. Receives response as var
mutationAttrs.error: Template ID to render on error. Receives error as var
mutationAttrs.loading: Template ID to show during request
mutationAttrs.confirm: Show browser confirm() dialog before sending
mutationAttrs.redirect: URL to navigate to on success (SPA route)
mutationAttrs.into: Write response to a named global store
mutationAttrs.cached: Cache responses (memory/local/session). Note: caching only applies to GET requests.

query.title: query — Read Requests with a Body (RFC 10008)
query.text: The query directive issues an HTTP QUERY request — a safe, idempotent, cacheable read that carries a request body. It renders, caches, and auto-fires like get, but sends a body like post. Use it when a search or filter is too complex for a URL query string but is still a read, not a mutation. No CSRF token is injected, since QUERY is a safe method.
query.cacheNote: When cached, the cache key includes the request body — so two QUERY requests to the same URL with different bodies are cached separately. Pagination (get-cursor / get-page / get-insert) remains GET-only.

urlEncoding.title: URL Interpolation and Encoding
urlEncoding.text: Since v1.10, all {placeholder} values in URL attributes are automatically passed through encodeURIComponent(). This is safe for query parameters and single-level path segments, but will break multi-segment paths that contain slashes — because / becomes %2F. If your value contains path separators, concatenate outside the braces instead.

skeleton.title: Skeleton Placeholders
skeleton.text: The skeleton attribute references a DOM element (by its id) that acts as a placeholder while the request is in flight. Unlike loading=, the skeleton element lives in the page markup from the start — making it SSG-friendly and preventing Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
skeleton.hiddenWhen: The skeleton is automatically hidden (via display: none) when the response arrives, when a non-GET method is used, or when the response is served from cache.
skeleton.combinedText: You can use both skeleton= and loading= on the same element. The skeleton shows first (pre-rendered), then hides when the request starts and the loading template takes over until the response arrives.
skeleton.skeletonContent: Pre-rendered HTML element in the page
skeleton.loadingContent: Template injected by No.JS at runtime
skeleton.skeletonVisibility: Visible on page load, hidden after response
skeleton.loadingVisibility: Shown during request, hidden after response
skeleton.skeletonCLS: Yes — occupies space before JS runs
skeleton.visibilityText: Do not hide the skeleton with CSS (display: none or a .hidden class). No.JS manages visibility by toggling the inline display style — if your CSS hides the element first, the show/hide logic will conflict.

pagination.title: Pagination & Infinite Scroll
pagination.text: No.JS supports declarative pagination and infinite scroll through a set of get-* attributes. Add get-trigger="scroll" for infinite scroll or get-trigger="click" for a load-more button, combined with get-insert="append" to accumulate results.
pagination.seeAlso: For the complete pagination reference — including cursor-based pagination, custom trigger labels, threshold configuration, and the fetch:end event — see the Pagination & Infinite Scroll dedicated page.

interceptors.title: Interceptors
interceptors.text: Interceptors let you hook into every HTTP request and response made by No.JS. Use them for authentication headers, global error handling, caching, logging, or request cancellation.
interceptors.asyncText: Interceptors can be async. No.JS awaits each interceptor with a 5-second timeout — if the interceptor doesn't resolve in time, it is skipped and a warning is logged.
interceptors.cancelText: Return an object with [NoJS.CANCEL]: true from a request interceptor to abort the request entirely. The fetch directive treats this as an AbortError.
interceptors.respondText: Return an object with [NoJS.RESPOND]: data from a request interceptor to skip the HTTP call and use the provided data directly as the response.
interceptors.replaceText: Return an object with [NoJS.REPLACE]: data from a response interceptor to replace the parsed body with your own data.
interceptors.redactionText: For security, No.JS automatically redacts sensitive headers (Authorization, X-API-Key, Cookie, etc.) in debug logs and when passing request data to untrusted interceptors. Only interceptors registered with { trusted: true } receive the original headers.
interceptors.respondEffect: Skips HTTP call, returns provided data as response
interceptors.replaceEffect: Replaces the parsed response body with provided data

context.title: $route — Route Context
context.matched: Whether an explicit route matched (true) or a wildcard/fallback is rendering (false)

programmatic.title: Programmatic Navigation
programmatic.callout: $router.push() and $router.replace() return Promises — navigation (including remote template loading) is fully async. In on:click handlers the return value is ignored, but in scripts you can await them:

remoteTemplates.title: Remote Templates in Routes
remoteTemplates.text1: Route templates can include <template src="..."> to load content from external files. They are automatically resolved before the route renders:
remoteTemplates.text2: Nested remote templates (a remote template that itself contains more <template src>) are recursively loaded.

fileBased.title: File-Based Routing
fileBased.text: Instead of declaring each route template manually, point your route-view outlet at a folder. No.JS will automatically resolve route paths to template files inside that folder.
fileBased.list1: Add route-view to your outlet element — file-based routing is enabled by default (config router.templates: "pages"). Override per-outlet with src="folder/".
fileBased.list2: When a user navigates to /analytics, No.JS resolves it to pages/analytics.tpl
fileBased.list3: The template is fetched, cached, and rendered — automatically
fileBased.srcDesc: Base folder for template resolution (per-outlet override; config: router.templates)
fileBased.extDesc: File extension appended to route segments (fallback: ".html")
fileBased.i18nNsDesc: When present, auto-derives i18n namespace from filename
fileBased.callout: Config default: The default router.templates is "pages", so file-based routing works out of the box — just add route-view to your outlet. Override with NoJS.config({ router: { templates: 'views' } }) or per-outlet via src="./custom/".
fileBased.exampleText: That's it — two lines for a full SPA with six routes.
fileBased.mixingText: Explicit <template route="..."> declarations always take priority. This lets you combine both approaches — use file-based routing for simple pages and explicit templates for routes that need guards, params, or named outlets:
fileBased.autoI18nText: When the route-view element has an i18n-ns attribute (even without a value), No.JS automatically loads the i18n namespace matching the filename:
fileBased.autoI18nText2: This replaces the need to add i18n-ns="..." on each route template individually.

lazyLoading.title: Lazy Template Loading
lazyLoading.text: The lazy attribute on <template src="..."> controls when a remote template is fetched relative to the first render. Use it to prioritise critical templates and defer heavy or rarely-visited pages.
lazyLoading.absentDesc: Auto: non-route templates and the active route template load before first render (Phase 1); other route templates preload in the background after first render (Phase 2).
lazyLoading.priorityDesc: Load before everything else — even before regular content includes. Use for critical shared layout templates.
lazyLoading.ondemandDesc: Only valid on route templates. Never preloaded — fetched the first time the user navigates to that route. Ideal for heavy or rarely-visited pages.

anchor.title: Anchor Links
anchor.text1: When using useHash: true, the URL hash (#) is used for routing (e.g. #/docs). This normally conflicts with standard anchor links like <a href="#section"> — but No.JS handles it automatically in both hash and history modes.
anchor.text2: Anchor links that point to an element id on the page are intercepted by the router: the target element is scrolled into view smoothly, and the clicked link receives an active class. The route itself is not affected.
anchor.list1: Clicking <a href="#introduction"> scrolls to <div id="introduction"> with smooth behavior
anchor.list2: The .active class is toggled on the clicked link (and removed from siblings)
anchor.list3: The current route path is preserved — no navigation occurs
anchor.list4: Links with a route attribute are always treated as route navigation, not anchors
anchor.tip: Tip: Style the active anchor link with .active in your CSS — the router manages the class for you.

namedOutlets.title: Named Outlets (route-view)
namedOutlets.text: Multiple route-view outlets can coexist in the same page. Give each outlet a name via the attribute value, and point route templates at specific outlets using the outlet attribute.
namedOutlets.callout: Outlets with no matching template for the current route are always cleared on navigation.

catchAll.title: 404 / Catch-All Routes
catchAll.text: Use route="*" to define a wildcard catch-all template that renders when no explicit route matches the current path. The wildcard is always evaluated last, regardless of DOM order.
catchAll.text2: Explicit routes always take priority — the wildcard only fires when matchRoute() returns no match.
catchAll.fallbackText: If you don't define a route="*" template, No.JS automatically shows a minimal built-in 404 page when no route matches. This ensures users always see something meaningful instead of a blank outlet.
catchAll.fallbackTip: The built-in fallback is intentionally minimal and unstyled. Define your own route="*" template for production apps.
catchAll.namedText: Each named outlet can have its own wildcard fallback. When no route matches for an outlet, the framework resolves fallbacks in this order:
catchAll.namedList1: Local wildcard — <template route="*" outlet="{name}"> for that specific outlet
catchAll.namedList2: Global wildcard — <template route="*"> (the default outlet's wildcard), used only for non-default outlets
catchAll.namedText2: If the sidebar has no local wildcard, it falls back to the global route="*". If neither exists, the built-in 404 is used.
catchAll.matchedText: The $route.matched boolean tells you whether the current path hit an explicit route (true) or a wildcard/fallback (false). Use it for conditional rendering inside your templates:
catchAll.matchedText2: $route.matched is set before the template renders, so it's always available during processing.
catchAll.remoteText: Wildcard routes support all the same attributes as regular route templates, including src for remote loading:
catchAll.remoteText2: The remote template is fetched, cached, and rendered just like any other route template — and it has full access to $route.path, $route.matched, and all other framework features.
catchAll.fileBasedText: When using file-based routing, navigating to a path whose .tpl file doesn't exist on the server (HTTP 404 or other error) automatically triggers the wildcard fallback chain.
catchAll.fileBasedText2: The failed HTTP response is not cached — subsequent navigations to other paths are unaffected.

headAttributes.title: Route Head Attributes
headAttributes.text: Route templates can declare page-title and page-description directly on the <template> tag. When the route activates, the corresponding <head> tags are updated automatically.
headAttributes.text2: Both static strings and dynamic expressions are supported:
headAttributes.pageTitleDesc: Sets document.title when the route is active
headAttributes.pageDescriptionDesc: Sets the <meta name="description"> content when the route is active
headAttributes.pageCanonicalDesc: Sets the <link rel="canonical"> href when the route is active
headAttributes.pageJsonldDesc: Injects a <script type="application/ld+json"> block into the document head
headAttributes.callout: For full head management (canonical URLs, JSON-LD, Open Graph), see the Head Management guide.

deployment.title: Deployment
deployment.text: No.JS uses the HTML5 History API by default (useHash: false), which produces clean URLs like /about and /products/42. These URLs require your server to serve the same index.html for every route — otherwise a direct visit to a client-side route returns a 404 from the server.
deployment.apacheText: Create a .htaccess file in your app's root:
deployment.netlifyText: Create a _redirects file in your publish directory:
deployment.vercelText: Create a vercel.json in your project root:
deployment.cloudflareText: Create a _redirects file in your project's output directory:
deployment.hashText: If you cannot configure your server (e.g. static file hosting without rewrite rules), use hash mode. Hash mode produces URLs like https://your-site.com/#/about — these work on any server without configuration, but search engines do not index hash fragments as separate pages.
deployment.hashCallout: No.JS emits a console warning when useHash: true is detected. To suppress it, add suppressHashWarning: true to your router config.

focusBehavior.title: Accessibility — Focus Management
focusBehavior.text: After each navigation, No.JS moves focus to a predictable element inside the new route content. This ensures screen readers announce the page change and keyboard users land in a useful spot.
focusBehavior.text2: When focusBehavior is 'auto' (the default), the framework walks a priority list:
focusBehavior.priority1: [autofocus] — an element with the autofocus attribute inside the route template
focusBehavior.priority2: h1 — the first <h1> inside the route content
focusBehavior.defaultText: Focus management is enabled by default — you don't need to configure anything. The 'auto' strategy works out of the box.
focusBehavior.timingText: Focus is moved after the route template is fully rendered and all remote templates inside it are resolved. This guarantees the target element exists in the DOM.
focusBehavior.sideEffectsText: The focused element receives a tabindex="-1" if it doesn't already have a tabindex, and the page scrolls to bring it into view via scrollIntoView({ block: 'nearest' }).
focusBehavior.futureText: Currently only 'auto' is supported. Future versions may add 'none' (opt out) and 'target' (focus a specific selector).
focusBehavior.ariaLiveText: For screen readers that don't respond to focus changes, add aria-live="polite" to your route-view. The browser will announce new content as it appears:

viewTransitions.title: View Transitions
viewTransitions.text: The transition attribute on route-view now uses the View Transition API by default. Just pick a preset name and route changes are animated natively by the browser — no manual CSS required.
viewTransitions.presetsText: No.JS ships with four built-in transition presets:
viewTransitions.presetSlide: Directional slide — content slides left/right based on navigation direction (forward/backward)
viewTransitions.presetFade: Crossfade — old content fades out while new content fades in
viewTransitions.presetScale: Scale zoom — old content scales down while new content scales up
viewTransitions.presetNone: Instant swap — no animation, content replaces immediately
viewTransitions.configText: The View Transition API is enabled by default via router.viewTransition: true. Set it to false to fall back to the legacy class-based transition system.
viewTransitions.customCssText: For custom animations, target the ::view-transition-old(route-content) and ::view-transition-new(route-content) pseudo-elements. The view-transition-name: route-content is set automatically on any outlet with a transition attribute.
viewTransitions.howItWorksText: When a route change occurs with a transition preset:
viewTransitions.howStep1: The router detects navigation direction (forward or backward) from the history stack
viewTransitions.howStep2: document.startViewTransition() is called, capturing the current outlet state
viewTransitions.howStep3: The new route content is rendered inside the outlet
viewTransitions.howStep4: The browser animates between old and new snapshots using the preset CSS rules
viewTransitions.deprecationText: The old class-based transition system (*-enter, *-enter-active, *-leave, etc.) on route-view is deprecated. It still works when router.viewTransition is set to false, but the View Transition API is the recommended approach going forward.
viewTransitions.deprecationCallout: Migration is mostly automatic. If you already use transition="fade" on a route-view, it now uses the View Transition API by default — no code changes needed. Your custom .fade-enter / .fade-leave CSS is simply ignored (unless you set viewTransition: false).

perRuleErrors.title: Per-Rule Error Messages
perRuleErrors.text: Use error-{rule} attributes to set a custom message for a specific rule, or error for a generic fallback.

errorTemplates.title: Error Templates
errorTemplates.text: Point an error attribute to a <template> using a # prefix to render rich error UI. Inside the template, $error contains the message and $rule the failing rule name.

errorClass.title: Error CSS Class
errorClass.text: Use error-class on the form or on individual fields to toggle a CSS class when a field is invalid and touched.

formContext.title: $form — Form Context
formContext.text: Inside any <form> with the validate attribute, $form provides:
formContext.valid: true if all fields pass validation
formContext.dirty: true if any field has been modified
formContext.touched: true if any field has been focused and blurred
formContext.submitting: true while the request is in flight
formContext.pending: true while async validators are running
formContext.errors: Map of field names → error messages
formContext.firstError: Error message of the first invalid field (DOM order)
formContext.errorCount: Number of fields currently failing validation
formContext.fields: Per-field state object (valid, error, dirty, touched)
formContext.reset: Reset form to initial values, clear errors and classes

formFields.title: $form.fields — Per-Field State
formFields.text: $form.fields exposes individual field state keyed by field name.
formFields.asText: Use the as attribute to expose a field's state under a custom name in the context.

validateOn.title: Validation Triggers (validate-on)
validateOn.text: By default, validation runs on input and focusout. Use validate-on to change when visual feedback appears.
validateOn.note: Internally, $form data is always kept up-to-date regardless of validate-on. The trigger only controls when visual feedback is shown.

validateIf.title: Conditional Validation (validate-if)
validateIf.text: Skip validation for a field based on a condition. When validate-if evaluates to false, the field is treated as valid.

autoDisable.title: Auto-Disable Submit Buttons
autoDisable.text: Submit buttons are automatically disabled when the form is invalid. Buttons with type="button" are not affected.

classToggle.title: class-* — Toggle Classes
classToggle.text: Add or remove CSS classes reactively based on expressions. Use class-{name} for individual toggles or class-map for multiple classes at once.
classToggle.multiObjectText: Use class-map with an object expression to toggle multiple classes. Keys are class names, values are boolean expressions.

classStatic.title: Static class Interaction
classStatic.text: class-* directives work alongside static class attributes. No.JS only toggles the directive-managed classes — static classes are never removed.
classStatic.callout: You can safely combine class="card" with class-active="isActive". The card class is always present; active is toggled.

inlineStyles.title: style-* — Inline Styles
inlineStyles.text: Set inline CSS properties reactively using style-{property} attributes. Property names use kebab-case (e.g. style-font-size, style-background-color).
inlineStyles.fromObjectText: Use style-map with an object expression to set multiple inline styles at once.

cssCustomProperties.title: CSS Custom Properties
cssCustomProperties.text: Use style-* with CSS custom properties (variables) for theme-aware reactive styling. Property names follow the same kebab-case convention.
cssCustomProperties.callout: CSS custom properties set via style-* are scoped to the element and inherited by its children — just like regular CSS custom properties.

enterLeave.title: animate — Enter/Leave Animations
enterLeave.row1: CSS animation class added when the element enters
enterLeave.row2: CSS animation class added when the element leaves
enterLeave.row3: Duration in milliseconds forwarded to animationDuration and used as the fallback timeout. When omitted the fallback fires on the next event-loop tick (0 ms)
enterLeave.row4: Delay in milliseconds between each item in a loop (each / foreach)
enterLeave.fallbackCallout: Fallback timeout — No.JS listens for animationend / transitionend to clean up classes and trigger re-renders after leave animations. If the event never fires (e.g. CSS is absent, element is detached) a setTimeout safety net ensures the pipeline is never permanently stalled. When animate-duration is omitted the timeout is 0 ms — it fires on the next event-loop tick with no artificial wait. Passing an explicit animate-duration="300" sets both animation-duration on the target element and the safety-net timeout to 300 ms.

transition.title: transition — CSS Transition Classes
transition.viewTransitionNote: Note: Route transitions on route-view now use the View Transition API by default. The class-based transition attribute described below still applies to regular elements (e.g. if, show).
transition.text1: Follows a convention similar to Vue's transition system.
transition.text2: No.JS adds/removes classes during the transition:

loopAnimations.title: Loop Animations
loopAnimations.text: All loop directives (foreach, each, for) support enter/leave animations and stagger.

builtIn.title: Built-in Animation Names
builtIn.text: No.JS ships with these CSS animations:

a11y.title: Accessibility — Reduced Motion
a11y.text: No.JS automatically respects the prefers-reduced-motion media query. When the user prefers reduced motion, all CSS animations are disabled via a built-in CSS rule that sets animation-duration and transition-duration to 0.01ms.
a11y.callout: This applies globally — no per-element configuration needed. Users who prefer reduced motion see instant state changes with no animation.

viewTransitions.title: View Transitions (Route Navigation)
viewTransitions.text: No.JS uses the View Transition API for smooth route transitions. Add a transition attribute to your route-view outlet to enable browser-native animated navigation between routes.
viewTransitions.presetFade: Crossfade between old and new content
viewTransitions.routingCallout: View Transitions offer more options including custom CSS, accessibility, and configuration details. See the Routing — View Transitions section for the full reference.

drag.title: drag — Make an Element Draggable
drag.dragTypeDesc: Named type — only matching drop-accept zones respond
drag.dragHandleDesc: Restricts grab area to a child element
drag.dragImageOffsetDesc: Pixel offset for custom drag image
drag.dragGhostClassDesc: Class added to the drag image element

drop.title: drop — Define a Drop Zone
drop.dropClassDesc: Class added when valid item hovers
drop.dropRejectClassDesc: Class added when item is rejected (wrong type or max exceeded)
drop.dropSettleClassDesc: Custom CSS class for the settle animation
drop.dropEmptyClassDesc: Custom CSS class for empty state on drop zone

dragList.title: drag-list — Sortable List
dragList.text: A higher-level directive that combines drag and drop into a sortable list bound to a state array.
dragList.dragListKeyDesc: Unique key per item for stable identity
dragList.dropSettleClassDesc: Custom CSS class for the settle animation
dragList.dropEmptyClassDesc: Custom CSS class for empty state on drag-list
dragList.dropPlaceholderDesc: Shows a placeholder where the item will be dropped

dragMultiple.title: drag-multiple — Multi-Select
dragMultiple.text: Enable click-to-select on child elements, then drag all selected items at once.
dragMultiple.demoText: Click items to select, <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+click for multi, then drag to the drop zone:
dragMultiple.dragGroupDesc: Group name — all selected items move together
dragMultiple.selDragResult: $drag becomes an array of all selected items

implicitVars.title: Implicit Variables
implicitVars.text: These variables are available inside drop expressions and on:drop handlers:
implicitVars.dragDesc: The dragged value. Array if multi-select
implicitVars.dropIndexDesc: Insertion index within the drop zone

cssClasses.title: CSS Classes
cssClasses.draggingDesc: On the source element while dragging
cssClasses.dragOverDesc: On the drop zone while a valid item hovers
cssClasses.dropRejectDesc: On the drop zone when the item is rejected (wrong type or max exceeded)
cssClasses.dragListEmptyDesc: On a drag-list when it has no items
cssClasses.demoText: Drag an item and watch the classes apply:

a11y.title: Accessibility
a11y.text: No.JS automatically adds ARIA attributes and keyboard support:
a11y.demoText: Use <kbd>Tab</kbd> to focus, <kbd>Space</kbd> to grab, <kbd>↑↓</kbd> to move, <kbd>Enter</kbd> to drop:

referenceTable.title: Complete Filter Reference
referenceTable.text: All 32 built-in filters at a glance:
referenceTable.capitalize: Capitalizes first letter of each word
referenceTable.truncate: Truncates text to N characters with ellipsis
referenceTable.pluck: Extracts a property from each item
referenceTable.first: Returns the first element of an array
referenceTable.last: Returns the last element of an array
referenceTable.relative: Time elapsed since date (e.g. '5 minutes ago')
referenceTable.default: Returns fallback if value is null/undefined
referenceTable.count: Returns the length of an array
referenceTable.join: Joins array elements with a separator
referenceTable.stripHtml: Removes HTML tags from a string
referenceTable.debug: Outputs value to console and returns it

externalFiles.title: External Locale Files
externalFiles.text: Instead of inlining all translations in JavaScript, you can load them from external JSON files. This is ideal for large apps with many locales or when translations are managed by a separate tool.
externalFiles.nsText: Split translations by feature for code-splitting and on-demand loading:
externalFiles.nsRouteText: Use i18n-ns on a route template to load a namespace on-demand when the route is navigated to:
externalFiles.nsElementText: Use i18n-ns on any element to load a namespace before its children are processed:
externalFiles.cachingText: Fetched JSON files are cached in memory by default. Set cache: false during development:

tHtml.title: HTML Translations (t-html)
tHtml.text: Add the t-html attribute to render a translation value as sanitized HTML instead of plain text. This is useful when translations contain markup such as links, bold text, or other inline HTML.
tHtml.callout: The HTML output is passed through _sanitizeHtml() to prevent XSS. Dangerous tags and attributes are stripped automatically.

fallback.title: Fallback Behavior
fallback.text: When a translation key is missing in the current locale, No.JS falls back to the fallbackLocale (default: same as defaultLocale). If the key is missing in both, the raw key path is displayed.
fallback.callout: Set fallbackLocale explicitly to ensure users always see meaningful text, even for partially translated locales.

detection.title: Browser Locale Detection
detection.text: Enable automatic locale detection from the browser's navigator.language setting with detectBrowser: true. The detected locale is matched against your available locales.
detection.supportedText: When loading locales lazily with loadPath, list the codes you ship in supportedLocales so detection can match the browser language before any file is fetched. First-visit priority: a persisted nojs-locale, then the detected browser language, then defaultLocale.
detection.callout: When combined with persist: true, the detected locale is saved so subsequent visits use the same language without re-detecting.

call.title: call — Trigger API Requests from Any Element

callAttrs.title: Attributes
callAttrs.text: call supports the same attributes as the HTTP directives (get, post, etc.), including loading templates, redirect, custom headers, and more.
callAttrs.headers: JSON string of additional request headers
callAttrs.as: Name to assign the response in the context. Default: "data"
callAttrs.into: Write response to a named global store
callAttrs.loading: Template ID to show during the request (e.g. "#spinner")
callAttrs.error: Template ID to render on error. Receives error via var
callAttrs.success: Template ID to render on success. Receives response via var
callAttrs.redirect: SPA route to navigate to on success
callAttrs.confirm: Show browser confirm() dialog before sending

loadingTpl.title: Loading Template
loadingTpl.text: Show a loading indicator while the request is in flight. The element is disabled during loading and its content is restored afterwards.

customHeaders.title: Custom Headers
customHeaders.text: Pass per-request headers as a JSON string:

redirectAfter.title: Redirect After Success
redirectAfter.text: Navigate to an SPA route after a successful request:

abort.title: Abort / SwitchMap
abort.text: Rapid clicks automatically abort the previous in-flight request before starting a new one, preventing race conditions. Only the result of the last click is applied.

events.title: Events
events.text: call emits events on the NoJS event bus (listen with NoJS.on(...)):
events.fetchEnd: { url } when the request completes (success or failure)

triggerAttrs.title: Trigger Attributes
triggerAttrs.text: Customize trigger behavior with additional attributes:
triggerAttrs.triggerData: Data payload attached to the CustomEvent's detail property

refsMap.title: $refs — Ref Map
refsMap.text: All elements with ref are accessible via $refs in the current scope:

priority.title: Priority Levels
priority.text: Priority determines when your directive runs relative to built-in directives. Lower numbers run first.

disposal.title: Disposal & Cleanup
disposal.text: Custom directives must clean up after themselves. Use the _onDispose callback to remove event listeners, clear timers, and unsubscribe watchers.
disposal.callout: Directives registered after init (via plugins) are not frozen — but they cannot override built-in directives.

webComponents.title: Web Components Compatibility
webComponents.text: No.JS directives work on custom elements:

retry.title: Retry Behavior
retry.text: Failed HTTP requests (5xx errors and network failures) are automatically retried. Configure the number of retries per element with the retry attribute, and the delay between retries with retry-delay.
retry.callout: Retries only apply to server errors (5xx) and network failures. Client errors (4xx) are not retried.

errorBoundary.title: error-boundary — Catch Errors in Subtree
errorBoundary.text: Wrap a section of your page with error-boundary to catch errors that occur within its subtree. The boundary intercepts two kinds of errors:
errorBoundary.type1: Expression evaluation errors — dispatched as nojs:error CustomEvents that bubble up from handler expressions (e.g. a bind or on:click expression throws).
errorBoundary.type2: Window-level errors — uncaught JS errors and resource load failures (e.g. an 404) that originate from elements inside the boundary.
errorBoundary.httpCaveat: error-boundary does NOT catch failed HTTP requests made by get/post/put/patch/delete directives. Use the error attribute on the fetch element for per-request error handling, or NoJS.on('fetch:error', ...) for global HTTP error handling.

boundaryEvents.title: Error Boundary Events
boundaryEvents.text: When an error boundary catches an error, it dispatches a nojs:error CustomEvent on the boundary element. Listen with on:error to log errors or show notifications.
boundaryEvents.callout: The $event.detail object contains: message (string), source (element), and error (original Error object).

expressionErrors.title: Expression Errors
expressionErrors.text: When an expression fails to evaluate (e.g. accessing a property of undefined), No.JS catches the error, logs a warning via _warn(), and returns undefined. One broken expression never crashes the entire page.
expressionErrors.callout: Enable NoJS.config({ debug: true }) to see detailed expression evaluation warnings in the console.

configStores.title: Pre-initializing Stores
configStores.text: Use the stores key in NoJS.config() to pre-create named global stores with initial data. Each entry becomes a reactive store accessible via $store.name anywhere in your markup.
configStores.callout: Stores created via config() will not overwrite stores that already exist — the first definition wins.

configOptions.title: Config Option Details
configOptions.sanitizeText: Controls whether HTML content rendered via bind-html is sanitized through a DOMParser-based structural sanitizer. When enabled, all potentially dangerous tags and attributes (e.g., <script>, onerror) are stripped before insertion into the DOM.
configOptions.devtoolsText: Enables the No.JS devtools panel, accessible via window.__NOJS_DEVTOOLS__. When active, it exposes reactive state, registered directives, active routes, and component trees for inspection in the browser console.
configOptions.templatesCacheText1: Controls whether the HTML content of remotely-fetched .tpl files is stored in an in-memory Map after the first request. On repeated navigations to the same route, the cached HTML is used directly and no HTTP request is made. The cache lives for the duration of the page session (no TTL — template assets are static).
configOptions.templatesCacheText2: Set to false during local development if you want changes to .tpl files to be reflected without a hard page reload.
configOptions.loadPathText: URL template for loading locale JSON files via fetch. Use {locale} and optionally {ns} as placeholders. When null, translations must be provided inline via NoJS.i18n({ locales }).
configOptions.nsText: Array of namespace identifiers to preload at init(). Each namespace corresponds to a separate JSON file per locale. Additional namespaces can be loaded on-demand via the i18n-ns directive or route attribute.
configOptions.cacheText: Controls whether fetched locale JSON files are stored in an in-memory Map after the first request. Set to false during development for hot-reload of translation files.
configOptions.i18nPersistText: When true, the currently selected locale is persisted to localStorage. On the next page load, the persisted locale is restored automatically — useful for remembering a user's language preference across sessions.
configOptions.exprCacheSizeText: Maximum number of entries in each of the two internal LRU caches used by the expression evaluator: one for parsed expression ASTs and one for parsed statement ASTs. When the limit is reached the least-recently-used entry is evicted. Increase it if your app evaluates a large number of distinct template expressions; decrease it to reduce memory pressure in constrained environments.
configOptions.maxEventListenersText: Maximum number of listener functions allowed per event name on the NoJS event bus (NoJS.on()). When the limit is reached, a MaxListenersExceeded warning is logged and the new listener is still registered. Increase this if your application legitimately uses many listeners for the same event.
configOptions.appIdText: An optional application identifier. Exposed via the devtools panel for distinguishing between multiple NoJS instances or environments.
configOptions.sanitizeHtmlText: Custom sanitizer hook for bind-html content. When set to a function, the built-in sanitizer is bypassed entirely and the provided function is used for all bind-html content. Useful for integrating external sanitizers like DOMPurify.
configOptions.dangerouslyDisableSanitizeText: When true, ALL sanitization of bind-html content is bypassed. This is not recommended — it exposes your application to XSS attacks. Use sanitizeHtml to provide a custom sanitizer instead.
configOptions.sanitizeLockCallout: The sanitize, dangerouslyDisableSanitize, and sanitizeHtml config keys are locked after init() completes. Calling NoJS.config() after initialization will not change these values — this prevents plugins or late-running scripts from weakening the security posture.
configOptions.suppressHashWarningText: When using history mode (useHash: false), NoJS logs a console warning if it detects a hash fragment in the URL that looks like a route path. Set this to true to suppress that warning.
configOptions.focusBehaviorText: Controls focus management after route navigation. When set to 'auto', NoJS moves focus to a predictable element inside the new route content (autofocus, h1, tabindex, or the outlet itself). Set to 'none' to disable.
configOptions.viewTransitionText: Controls whether route transitions use the View Transition API. When true (default), route changes are animated natively by the browser using built-in presets (slide, fade, scale, none). Set to false to fall back to the legacy class-based transition system.

apiProperties.title: API Properties
apiProperties.baseApiUrlText: Getter/setter for the base API URL used by all fetch directives and NoJS.http calls. Can be read or reassigned at runtime.
apiProperties.versionText: Read-only property that returns the current No.JS framework version string.

security.title: Security
security.xssList1: bind always sets textContent, never innerHTML — safe by default.
security.xssList2: bind-html sanitizes content using a built-in DOMParser-based structural sanitizer (strips <script> tags, blocks on* event handlers, removes javascript: URIs).
security.xssList3: Template expressions are evaluated by a custom sandboxed parser — no eval() or Function() is used, and dangerous properties like __proto__ and constructor are blocked.
security.cspSecText1: No.JS uses a custom expression parser that is fully CSP-compliant — no eval() or Function() constructor is used. No unsafe-eval directive is required in your Content Security Policy.

use.title: NoJS.use()
use.text: Register a plugin before or after NoJS.init(). If the app is already initialized, the plugin's init hook runs immediately.
use.objectFormText: The standard way to define a plugin. Provide a name, an install function, and optional lifecycle hooks.
use.functionText: For simple plugins, pass a named function. The function name becomes the plugin name.
use.functionCallout: Anonymous functions and arrow functions are rejected — the plugin must have a name.
use.optionsText: The second argument to NoJS.use() is passed to the plugin's install function.
use.optionsTrustedNote: The trusted option is special — see Trusted Interceptors below.

interface.title: Plugin Interface
interface.nameDesc: Unique identifier. Duplicate names are rejected.
interface.initDesc: Called after NoJS.init() completes (DOM is ready).
interface.lifecycleInstall: install runs immediately and synchronously. Use it to register interceptors, globals, directives, event listeners, and stores.
interface.lifecycleInit: init runs after the DOM is processed and the router is active. Use it for work that depends on rendered elements or route state.
interface.lifecycleDispose: dispose runs during app teardown. Use it to close WebSocket connections, clear intervals, flush pending analytics, etc.
interface.duplicateText: A plugin name can only be registered once. If NoJS.use() is called again with the same name but a different object, a warning is logged and the call is ignored.
interface.freezingText: Core directives (state, bind, get, on:*, etc.) are frozen after the framework loads. Plugins can register new directives but cannot override built-in ones.

globals.title: NoJS.global()
globals.text: Inject a reactive variable accessible as $name in any template expression.
globals.namingPrefix: Global names are accessed with a $ prefix in templates: NoJS.global('foo', ...) becomes $foo.
globals.namingNamespace: Use your plugin name as a namespace to avoid collisions: $analytics, $auth, $featureFlags.
globals.reservedText: The following names cannot be used with NoJS.global():
globals.reservedProto: Prototype pollution vectors (__proto__, constructor, prototype) are also blocked.
globals.reactivityText: Object values passed to NoJS.global() are automatically wrapped in a reactive context. Mutations trigger DOM updates just like store or state changes.
globals.ownershipText: When a plugin registers a global during its install phase, it becomes the owner. If a different plugin overwrites that global, a warning is logged.

sentinels.title: Interceptor Sentinels
sentinels.text: Request interceptors can return special objects keyed by sentinel Symbols to control the fetch pipeline.
sentinels.cancelText: Return an object with [NoJS.CANCEL]: true to prevent the request from being sent. The fetch throws an AbortError.
sentinels.respondText: Return an object with [NoJS.RESPOND]: data to short-circuit the request and return data directly as the response. No HTTP request is made.
sentinels.replaceText: Return an object with [NoJS.REPLACE]: data from a response interceptor to replace the parsed response body with custom data.
sentinels.respondEffect: Returns data directly, skips HTTP call

trusted.title: Trusted Interceptors
trusted.text: By default, interceptors receive redacted copies of requests and responses — sensitive headers and URL parameters are stripped or replaced with [REDACTED].
trusted.fullAccess: Auth plugins that need access to the real headers can be installed with { trusted: true }.
trusted.callout: A console warning is logged when a plugin is installed with trusted access. Only grant trusted to plugins you control or have audited.
trusted.requestLabel: Request headers stripped before passing to untrusted interceptors:
trusted.responseLabel: Response headers stripped from the response proxy:

dispose.title: NoJS.dispose()
dispose.text: Tears down the entire application: disposes plugins, clears globals, and removes interceptors.
dispose.orderStep1: Plugins are disposed in reverse installation order (last installed, first disposed).
dispose.orderStep2: Each plugin's dispose function is given a 3-second timeout. If it exceeds the timeout, an error is logged and disposal continues with the next plugin.
dispose.orderStep3: After all plugins are disposed, globals and interceptors are cleared.
dispose.asyncText: Plugin dispose functions can be async. The framework awaits each one (subject to the 3-second timeout).
dispose.callout: Plugins cannot be installed during disposal. Calls to NoJS.use() while dispose() is running are ignored with a warning.

security.title: Security Guidelines
security.text: When authoring plugins, follow these practices to keep your application secure.
security.namespaceText: Prefix globals, stores, and event names with your plugin name to avoid collisions.
security.evalText: Values passed to NoJS.global() are checked for dangerous references. eval and Function are blocked.
security.cleanupText: Always provide a dispose hook that clears intervals, closes connections, and removes event listeners.
security.overwriteText: If your plugin detects that a global is already owned by another plugin, consider using a different name rather than silently overwriting.
security.validateText: Check required options in install and warn early.

example.title: Complete Example
example.text: A full analytics plugin demonstrating the plugin lifecycle, globals, interceptors, and disposal.

intro.text: No.JS lets you control <head> metadata declaratively from anywhere in your markup. Use hidden elements with page-title, page-description, page-canonical, and page-jsonld attributes — they are evaluated reactively and update the document head automatically.
intro.routingTip: For route-level head management, you can also use page-title and page-description directly on <template route="..."> tags. See Route Head Attributes.

placement.title: Placement
placement.text: Place head-management elements inside any state scope. They must have the hidden attribute — they are never rendered visually.
placement.reactive: All values are reactive — when the underlying state changes, the document head is updated immediately.

pageTitle.title: page-title
pageTitle.text: Sets document.title to the evaluated expression.
pageTitle.expression: The value is a standard No.JS expression evaluated against the element's context.

pageDescription.title: page-description
pageDescription.text: Sets the content attribute of the <meta name="description"> tag. If the tag doesn't exist, it is created.
pageDescription.duplicate: Only one page-description should be active at a time. If multiple are present, the last one processed wins.

pageCanonical.title: page-canonical
pageCanonical.text: Sets the href attribute of the <link rel="canonical"> tag. If the tag doesn't exist, it is created.
pageCanonical.existing: If a canonical link already exists in your HTML, the attribute value is updated in place.

pageJsonld.title: page-jsonld
pageJsonld.text: Injects a <script type="application/ld+json"> block into the document head. The inner text of the element is used as the JSON-LD payload.
pageJsonld.scriptNote: The content is inserted as-is — make sure it is valid JSON. Use {variable} interpolation to inject dynamic values.
pageJsonld.marker: The generated script tag is marked with data-nojs so it can be identified and cleaned up on unmount.

notes.title: Notes & Edge Cases
notes.competingText: If multiple elements set the same head attribute (e.g. two page-title elements), the last one processed wins. When an element is unmounted, its head contribution is reverted to the previous value.
notes.cleanupText: When an element with a head directive is removed from the DOM (e.g. inside an if block or a route change), its head contribution is automatically cleaned up.
notes.captureText: The page-jsonld element's innerHTML is captured at processing time. If you need reactive JSON-LD, place the element inside a state scope and use {expression} interpolation.

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.text: Add get-trigger and get-insert to any get directive to enable pagination. Here is a minimal infinite scroll example:
quickStart.note: The get directive fetches page 1 on load. When the user scrolls near the bottom, it automatically fetches the next page and appends the new items.

attributes.title: Attributes Reference
attributes.getInsert: Controls where new content is placed relative to existing content. Without this attribute, fetched content replaces the current content entirely.
attributes.getTrigger: Controls when the fetch fires. Without this attribute, the fetch fires immediately on mount.
attributes.getPage: Initial page number for offset-based pagination. The page context variable increments automatically after each successful fetch.
attributes.getCursor: Enable cursor-based pagination. The cursor is extracted from the response header (X-Cursor) or JSON body field.
attributes.getCursorField: Custom JSON field path for cursor extraction using dot notation (e.g. pagination.nextToken). Without this, No.JS probes cursor, next_cursor, nextCursor, next.
attributes.getThreshold: IntersectionObserver rootMargin. Controls how early the fetch triggers before the element enters the viewport.
attributes.getTriggerLabel: Custom label text for the auto-generated load-more button when using get-trigger="button".

infiniteScroll.title: Infinite Scroll
infiniteScroll.desc: Automatically loads the next page when the user scrolls near the bottom of the list.
infiniteScroll.label: Live Result

loadMore.title: Load More Button
loadMore.desc: Displays a button that the user clicks to load the next page of results.
loadMore.label: Live Result

lazyLoad.title: Lazy Load on Visible
lazyLoad.desc: Content is fetched only when the element scrolls into the viewport. Ideal for below-the-fold sections.
lazyLoad.label: Live Result

hoverPrefetch.title: Hover Prefetch
hoverPrefetch.desc: Content is fetched on the first mouseenter event. Useful for tooltips and preview cards.
hoverPrefetch.label: Live Result
hoverPrefetch.hoverMe: Hover over this card to load preview content

programmatic.title: Programmatic Fetch
programmatic.desc: The fetch does not fire automatically. Use $refs to trigger it manually via a button or other event.
programmatic.label: Live Result
programmatic.clickToLoad: Click the button to trigger the fetch manually.

cursorPagination.title: Cursor Pagination
cursorPagination.desc: Uses cursor-based pagination instead of page numbers. The cursor is extracted from the JSON response body.
cursorPagination.label: Live Result

liveFeed.title: Live Feed (Prepend)
liveFeed.desc: New content is inserted at the top of the list, simulating a live feed or news ticker.
liveFeed.label: Live Result

matrix.title: Composition Matrix
matrix.text: The get-trigger and get-insert attributes combine to form a 5x3 matrix of behaviors. Here is how each combination works:
matrix.immediateReplace: Default behavior — fetch replaces content on mount.
matrix.immediateAppend: Fetch on mount, append result below existing content.
matrix.immediatePrepend: Fetch on mount, prepend result above existing content.
matrix.scrollReplace: Falls back to visible behavior (scroll requires insert mode).
matrix.scrollAppend: Infinite scroll — appends new pages as user scrolls down.
matrix.scrollPrepend: Infinite scroll — prepends new pages as user scrolls up.
matrix.buttonReplace: Falls back to immediate behavior (button requires insert mode).
matrix.buttonAppend: Load-more button — appends next page on click.
matrix.buttonPrepend: Load-more button — prepends next page on click.
matrix.visibleReplace: Lazy load — fetches and replaces content when element enters viewport.
matrix.visibleAppend: Lazy load — fetches and appends content when element enters viewport.
matrix.visiblePrepend: Lazy load — fetches and prepends content when element enters viewport.
matrix.hoverReplace: Prefetch — fetches and replaces content on first mouseenter.
matrix.hoverAppend: Prefetch — fetches and appends content on first mouseenter.
matrix.hoverPrepend: Prefetch — fetches and prepends content on first mouseenter.
matrix.noneReplace: Manual — no auto-fetch, use $refs.refresh() to trigger.

endOfData.title: End-of-Data Detection
endOfData.text: No.JS detects the end of paginated data in three ways, depending on the response format:
endOfData.emptyBodyText: When the server returns an empty response body (Content-Length: 0 or empty string), No.JS stops pagination and hides the load-more button.
endOfData.lastPageHeaderText: The server can send an X-NoJS-Last-Page: true response header to explicitly signal there are no more pages.
endOfData.nullCursorText: For cursor-based pagination, when the cursor field in the JSON response is null or absent, No.JS stops pagination.

templates.title: Templates with Pagination
templates.text: Loading, error, and empty templates work seamlessly with pagination. The loading template is shown during each page fetch, and the error template is shown if any page fails.
templates.loadingNote: During pagination (not the first fetch), a small inline loading indicator is shown at the insertion edge rather than replacing the entire content.
templates.emptyNote: The empty template is only shown when the first page returns no data. Subsequent empty pages simply stop pagination.

scrollPosition.title: Scroll Position Preservation
scrollPosition.text: When using get-insert="prepend", No.JS automatically preserves the user's scroll position. New content is inserted above the current viewport, and the scroll offset is adjusted so the user's view does not jump.

overview.title: Directive Compatibility
overview.text: No.JS processes directives by priority order (state 0 > HTTP 1 > computed/watch 2 > ref 5 > structural 10 > rendering/events 20 > validation 30). Most directives compose freely, but certain same-element combinations require awareness of processing order and scope mechanics. This page documents every known interaction.
overview.callout: Combinations marked "Defined semantics" work correctly but follow specific rules you should understand. Combinations marked "Use workaround" have a known limitation with a documented alternative pattern.

ifGates.title: if gates the entire element
ifGates.text: As of v1.18.0, if gates the entire element, not just its children. This means all directives on the same element respect the if condition:
ifGates.http: HTTP directives (get, post, etc.) on the same element only fire when the condition is true. Zero network requests while false.
ifGates.computed: Computed values and watch side effects on the same element only run when the condition is true. No phantom computations.
ifGates.head: Head directives (page-title, page-description, page-canonical, page-jsonld) only apply when the condition is true.
ifGates.binding: Binding, model, and event directives on the same element are gated in the same way.

perClone.title: Per-clone computed/watch in loops
perClone.text: When computed or watch appears on a loop element, each clone gets its own independent instance. A computed creates a per-item derived value scoped to that clone's context. A watch fires independently per clone when the watched property changes.

usePriority.title: use priority 9
usePriority.text: The use directive runs at priority 9 (just below structural directives at 10), ensuring templates are instantiated before if/each processing.

httpPlacement.title: HTTP verb placement
httpPlacement.text: HTTP directives (get, post, put, patch, delete) should be placed on a parent element, not on the loop element itself. The loop element is used as a clone template — placing an HTTP directive on it would fire one request per clone.

switchLoop.title: switch + loop on case children
switchLoop.text: Placing a loop directive (each/foreach/for) on a case child of a switch renders both branches; the switch logic goes inert. Workaround: wrap the loop in a container element inside the case.

ifLoop.title: if + loop on the same element
ifLoop.text: if and a loop directive on the same element cannot coordinate — the condition cannot remove items, and empty shells may render. Workaround: wrap the loop in a container element with the if.

elseIfLoop.title: else-if + loop on the same element
elseIfLoop.text: else-if combined with a loop on the same element breaks the conditional chain. Items render regardless of the preceding if condition. Additionally, a sibling else element is orphaned. Workaround: wrap the loop in a container element with the else-if.

duplicateRef.title: Duplicate ref in loop
duplicateRef.text: Using ref on a loop element means each clone overwrites the same ref name. Only the last clone's element is accessible via $refs. Workaround: use bind-ref with a dynamic expression to create unique ref names per clone.

bindValueModel.title: bind-value + model on the same input
bindValueModel.text: Both bind-value and model set up two-way binding pipelines with input listeners. The two handlers can fight over the value during typing, especially for number inputs. Workaround: use only model for two-way binding.

watchOnChange.title: watch + on:change on the same element
watchOnChange.text: Both watch and on:change claim the change event, leading to duplicate handler execution. Workaround: use only one mechanism — watch for reactive side effects or on:change for DOM event handling.

tBind.title: t + bind double writer
tBind.text: Both t (i18n translation) and bind write to the element's textContent. The attribute processed last wins. Workaround: use t with interpolation parameters (t-name, t-count) or use bind alone.

httpOnLoop.title: HTTP verb on loop element
httpOnLoop.text: Placing an HTTP directive (get, post, etc.) directly on the loop element causes one network request per clone, since clones retain the HTTP attribute. Workaround: place the HTTP directive on a parent element.

ifGates.title: if now gates the entire element
ifGates.before: Before v1.18.0, if only controlled children visibility. Directives with higher priority than if (such as HTTP at priority 1, computed/watch at priority 2, and head directives at priority 1) would still execute on the element even when the condition was false.
ifGates.after: After v1.18.0, if gates the entire element. All directives on the same element respect the if condition.
ifGates.check: If you relied on a falsy if branch to still compute values or fire HTTP requests, move those directives to a separate element without an if condition.

usePriority.title: use priority changed to 9
usePriority.text: The use directive now has an explicit priority of 9, placing it just below structural directives (10). This ensures templates are instantiated before if/each processing. This change is backward-compatible for standard usage.

sse.basicText: The sse directive opens an EventSource connection to a server endpoint and exposes incoming data as a reactive context variable via the as attribute. JSON payloads are parsed automatically; non-JSON data is stored as a plain string.
sse.text: When the server sends non-JSON data (plain text, CSV lines, etc.), the as variable receives the raw string.
sse.sse: URL of the SSE endpoint. Supports interpolated expressions (e.g. /api/{channel}).
sse.as: Name of the reactive context variable that receives each message's data.
sse.sseEvent: Named SSE event type to listen for. Without this, only unnamed (default) messages are captured.
sse.sseInsert: How new messages update the variable: replace (default), append (oldest-first array), or prepend (newest-first array).
sse.sseLimit: Maximum array length when using append or prepend mode. Oldest entries are dropped when the limit is exceeded.
sse.sseCredentials: Boolean attribute. When present, the EventSource is opened with withCredentials: true, sending cookies on cross-origin requests.
sse.into: Store name. When set, each message is also written to the named global store under the as key.
sse.error: ID of a <template> element to render when the connection permanently fails (readyState === CLOSED).
sse.then: Expression executed after each message is processed. Receives $event (the parsed data).
sse.insertModesText: The sse-insert attribute controls how successive messages update the context variable. Replace is the default and suits single-value streams like stock tickers. Append and prepend collect messages into an array, optionally capped by sse-limit.
sse.namedEventsText: By default, the directive listens for unnamed SSE messages (the message event). Set sse-event to listen for a specific named event type instead. Multiple elements can subscribe to different event types on the same endpoint — each opens its own EventSource connection.
sse.connectionStateText: Every sse element exposes a reactive $sse object on its context with three boolean properties reflecting the EventSource readyState.
sse.connecting: true while the EventSource is in CONNECTING state (initial connect or auto-reconnect).
sse.open: true when the connection is open and receiving messages.
sse.errorProp: true only when the connection has permanently failed (readyState === CLOSED). During auto-reconnect, error remains false.
sse.statesCallout: During auto-reconnect the browser's EventSource transitions to CONNECTING. NoJS mirrors this: $sse.connecting becomes true, $sse.open becomes false, and $sse.error stays false. The error flag only fires when the connection cannot be recovered (CLOSED).
sse.liveFeedText: Combine prepend insert mode, a limit, and connection-state indicators for a complete real-time activity feed with automatic reconnection status.
sse.reactiveUrlText: When the sse URL contains interpolated expressions, the directive watches for changes. If the resolved URL changes, the current EventSource is closed and a new one is opened to the updated endpoint. The as variable is reset.
sse.storeText: Add the into attribute to write each message into a global store alongside the local context variable. Other elements anywhere in the DOM can read the data via $store.storeName.
sse.errorHandlingText: Point the error attribute to a <template> to render fallback content when the connection permanently fails. The template receives the error object. During auto-reconnect, the original content remains visible — the error template only renders when readyState is CLOSED.
sse.thenText: The then attribute accepts an expression that runs after each message is processed and the context variable is updated. The parsed message data is available as $event. Use it for side effects like incrementing counters or triggering actions.
sse.noLoadingText: Unlike HTTP directives (get, post), the sse directive does not support a loading template attribute. SSE connections are long-lived — there is no single request/response cycle to bracket. Use $sse.connecting to show a status indicator while the connection is being established.
sse.authText: The EventSource API does not support custom headers. Two authentication strategies are available: query-string tokens and cookie-based credentials.
sse.authCallout: The sse-credentials attribute sets withCredentials: true on the EventSource, which sends cookies on cross-origin requests. The server must respond with the appropriate CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, explicit Access-Control-Allow-Origin).
sse.interceptorsNote: NoJS fetch interceptors do not apply to SSE connections. EventSource is a separate browser API — header injection, caching, and retry logic from the fetch pipeline are not available.
sse.connectionLimitText: Browsers enforce a per-origin limit of roughly 6 concurrent HTTP/1.1 connections. Each sse element opens one EventSource connection. NoJS logs a warning when a 6th connection to the same origin is opened. To stay within limits, consolidate related data into a single SSE endpoint with named events, or use HTTP/2 which multiplexes connections.
